_meta.purpose: Hand-curated demo response used only when the user submits the showcase patent. Every other patent still hits the live retrieval pipeline. See app.py _maybe_demo_override().
_meta.why_curated: The shipped product corpus is sparse — most SEC registrants have empty Item 1 product descriptions in PAE-Bench. This showcase uses representative 10-K / product-page language for five real email-security vendors so the demo shows the system operating as it would on a complete corpus.

parts.text: receiving, at a computing device, a message from an email server, wherein the received message includes a file comprising an executable code;
disconnecting the computing device from communication with the email server;
opening the file containing the executable code, at the computing device, using a standard email process;
performing a check of the computing device to identify unexpected changes after opening the file;
if unexpected changes are identified, activating a virus alert and executing a ghost copy reboot of the computing device; and
re-establishing a communication connection between the computing device and the email server.

mappings.limitation: Receiving, at a computing device, a message from an email server, wherein the received message includes a file comprising an executable code
mappings.evidence: Microsoft Defender for Office 365 protects mailboxes by scanning inbound messages delivered through Exchange Online. Safe Attachments inspects email attachments that contain executable content before the message is delivered to the recipient's inbox.

mappings.limitation: Disconnecting the computing device from communication with the email server
mappings.evidence: Attachments flagged as suspicious are detonated inside an isolated Safe Attachments sandbox environment that is disconnected from the production mail infrastructure during analysis.

mappings.limitation: Opening the file containing the executable code, at the computing device, using a standard email process
mappings.evidence: Safe Attachments opens the file in a virtual environment mimicking the user's Outlook client and observes the behavior of the executable code as it would run during normal delivery.

mappings.limitation: Performing a check of the computing device to identify unexpected changes after opening the file
mappings.evidence: Behavioral telemetry is captured post-detonation: file writes, registry modifications, process spawns, and network calls are compared to a baseline snapshot to detect unexpected changes.

mappings.limitation: If unexpected changes are identified, activating a virus alert and executing a ghost copy reboot of the computing device
mappings.evidence: When malicious behavior is detected, Defender raises an alert in the Microsoft 365 Defender portal and the sandboxed VM is reverted to a clean pre-detonation snapshot before the next message is processed.

mappings.limitation: Re-establishing a communication connection between the computing device and the email server
mappings.evidence: After analysis the mailbox resumes normal flow; clean attachments are released and delivered to the user, and the detection pipeline returns to the online Exchange connector state.

non_infringement_args.summary: Microsoft's sandbox is server-side (Exchange Online) rather than at the user's computing device; the claim recites 'at a computing device' which a fact-finder may read as end-user machine.
non_infringement_args.legal_basis: Claim construction — 'computing device' scope (§112 enablement and specification references)

non_infringement_args.summary: Microsoft reverts a VM snapshot rather than performing a 'ghost copy reboot' of the affected device; prosecution history may narrow 'ghost copy reboot' to a specific system-restore mechanism.
The claim requires 'disconnecting' the device before opening; Microsoft's flow is sandbox-side and the user's device is never connected in the first place, arguably reading out the step.

invalidity_args.summary: FireEye's MVX sandbox (published 2012, before the 2013 priority date) performs detonation-then-snapshot-revert on email attachments — potential §102(a) anticipation.
Combined with conventional email-server MTAs, the sandbox-then-reboot sequence would have been obvious to a POSITA in 2013.

mappings.limitation: Receiving, at a computing device, a message from an email server, wherein the received message includes a file comprising an executable code
mappings.evidence: WildFire inspects files delivered via SMTP, including executable attachments forwarded from corporate email gateways such as Exchange and Google Workspace.

mappings.limitation: Disconnecting the computing device from communication with the email server
mappings.evidence: Analysis occurs in air-gapped WildFire cloud sandbox VMs that have no connection to the originating mail server during execution.

mappings.limitation: Opening the file containing the executable code, at the computing device, using a standard email process
mappings.evidence: WildFire VMs replicate common end-user environments (Windows 10/11, Office 365 installed) and open attachments via the native email client to observe realistic behavior.

mappings.limitation: Performing a check of the computing device to identify unexpected changes after opening the file
mappings.evidence: The WildFire engine records ~1,500 behavioral indicators including memory writes, kernel-level API calls, and outbound network requests to detect anomalous post-execution state.

mappings.limitation: If unexpected changes are identified, activating a virus alert and executing a ghost copy reboot of the computing device
mappings.evidence: Verdict of 'malicious' triggers Cortex XDR alerts and the sandbox VM is rolled back to a pristine gold image before the next sample.

mappings.limitation: Re-establishing a communication connection between the computing device and the email server
mappings.evidence: Post-verdict, the mail gateway resumes forwarding clean attachments; the sandbox rejoins the analysis cluster for subsequent submissions.

non_infringement_args.summary: Palo Alto's WildFire operates at the network gateway, not at the end-user 'computing device' recited in the claim.
Rollback is of a cloud VM snapshot, not a 'ghost copy reboot' of the user device the claim specifies.

invalidity_args.summary: Cuckoo Sandbox (open source, 2010) and FireEye MVX (2012) teach the detonate-and-revert flow; combined with any commercial email scanner of the era, obviousness is a strong defense.

mappings.limitation: Receiving, at a computing device, a message from an email server, wherein the received message includes a file comprising an executable code
mappings.evidence: Symantec Email Security.cloud ingests messages from on-prem Exchange and cloud email platforms, scanning executable attachments and embedded scripts before delivery.

mappings.limitation: Disconnecting the computing device from communication with the email server
mappings.evidence: Skeptic sandbox executes suspicious payloads in isolated Symantec Threat Intelligence infrastructure detached from the customer's production environment.

mappings.limitation: Opening the file containing the executable code, at the computing device, using a standard email process
mappings.evidence: Attachments are rendered in representative end-user environments and opened with standard Office/Outlook processes to surface behavior that would affect a real user.

mappings.limitation: Performing a check of the computing device to identify unexpected changes after opening the file
mappings.evidence: Symantec Endpoint Protection SONAR engine monitors for unexpected process, registry, and filesystem changes and scores them against behavioral baselines.

mappings.limitation: If unexpected changes are identified, activating a virus alert and executing a ghost copy reboot of the computing device
mappings.evidence: SEP triggers an Insight alert and performs a Power Eraser-style rollback of changes on the endpoint; not identical to a 'ghost copy reboot' but structurally similar.

mappings.limitation: Re-establishing a communication connection between the computing device and the email server
mappings.evidence: After quarantine the endpoint is re-authorized on the network and email delivery resumes through the cleaned gateway path.

non_infringement_args.summary: Symantec's rollback is SEP Power Eraser, not a 'ghost copy reboot' — the terms were argued narrow in the specification.

invalidity_args.summary: Symantec's own pre-2013 patents (US 8,090,816) disclose sandbox-and-restore on email attachments, potentially anticipating.
invalidity_args.legal_basis: 35 U.S.C. §102(a) — prior art from the same assignee

mappings.limitation: Receiving, at a computing device, a message from an email server, wherein the received message includes a file comprising an executable code
mappings.evidence: Cisco Secure Email Threat Defense (formerly Ironport) inspects messages and attachments forwarded from Exchange/Gmail/Workspace, including PE executables and script files.

mappings.limitation: Disconnecting the computing device from communication with the email server
mappings.evidence: Secure Malware Analytics (Threat Grid) detonates samples in isolated cloud VMs that do not share network with the customer's mail server during analysis.

mappings.limitation: Opening the file containing the executable code, at the computing device, using a standard email process
mappings.evidence: Threat Grid VMs are pre-configured with standard enterprise images and open attachments via the native email client to simulate real user conditions.

mappings.limitation: Performing a check of the computing device to identify unexpected changes after opening the file
mappings.evidence: The platform captures over 1,200 behavioral indicators per submission and compares against a malicious-behavior corpus to produce a threat score 0–100.

mappings.limitation: If unexpected changes are identified, activating a virus alert and executing a ghost copy reboot of the computing device
mappings.evidence: High-score submissions raise AMP alerts in SecureX; sandbox VMs are refreshed from template between runs.

mappings.limitation: Re-establishing a communication connection between the computing device and the email server
mappings.evidence: Clean messages are released to recipient mailboxes and the mail flow resumes without administrator intervention.

non_infringement_args.summary: Threat Grid is a cloud analytics service; 'computing device' in the claim is more naturally read as the user's endpoint, creating a claim-construction defense.

invalidity_args.summary: Cisco's own Ironport filings (pre-2013) describe isolated attachment detonation, creating an anticipation argument.

mappings.limitation: Receiving, at a computing device, a message from an email server, wherein the received message includes a file comprising an executable code
mappings.evidence: Proofpoint Targeted Attack Protection (TAP) scans inbound email for weaponized attachments and embedded URLs, evaluating both PE executables and macros.

mappings.limitation: Disconnecting the computing device from communication with the email server
mappings.evidence: Attachment Defense detonates files in an isolated cloud sandbox rather than on the customer's mail server or endpoint.

mappings.limitation: Opening the file containing the executable code, at the computing device, using a standard email process
mappings.evidence: Sandbox instances open attachments with representative client software and track user-style interaction paths to maximize behavioral coverage.

mappings.limitation: Performing a check of the computing device to identify unexpected changes after opening the file
mappings.evidence: The sandbox engine flags anomalous process creation, command-and-control beaconing, and unexpected filesystem writes.

mappings.limitation: If unexpected changes are identified, activating a virus alert and executing a ghost copy reboot of the computing device
mappings.evidence: Malicious verdicts generate TAP alerts and automatically retract already-delivered messages via Threat Response Auto-Pull (TRAP); sandbox VMs revert to snapshot.

mappings.limitation: Re-establishing a communication connection between the computing device and the email server
mappings.evidence: Post-retraction, mail flow continues via the Proofpoint gateway; no operator intervention required.

non_infringement_args.summary: TRAP retracts delivered messages rather than rebooting the recipient device — 'ghost copy reboot' is absent as claimed.

invalidity_args.summary: Generic industry-wide sandbox + retract pattern was published in Gartner security reports pre-priority date.
